title: W2 C Open Sets
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  Week 2C    —    Term 1, 2026

                     Continuous Preferences


Theorem 1

 If preferences ⪰ on X = Rn
                          + are continuous, then for all x ∈ X, the

 sets I(x), N W T (x) and NBT (x) are all closed.

                             Open Intervals




The open interval Iϵ (x) = (x − ϵ, x + ϵ) contains all real numbers strictly
less than ϵ > 0 distant from x.

                               z

                               Open Intervals




The open interval Iϵ (x) = (x − ϵ, x + ϵ) contains all real numbers strictly
less than ϵ > 0 distant from x.

                                 z

                  x−ϵ                  x                x+ϵ


Pick an arbitrary z ̸= x in the open interval Iϵ (x).
It is possible to embed z in an open interval fully contained inside Iϵ (x).

          Open Intervals



z − ϵ′     z       z + ϵ′

x−ϵ            x            x+ϵ

                              Open Intervals



                z − ϵ′            z       z + ϵ′

                x−ϵ                   x            x+ϵ


◦ Let δ = |z − x| > 0.

◦ Now let ϵ′ = ϵ − δ > 0.

◦ Verify that Iϵ′ (z) ⊆ Iϵ (x).




                                                         5/16
                              Open Intervals



                z − ϵ′            z       z + ϵ′

                x−ϵ                   x                   x+ϵ


◦ Let δ = |z − x| > 0.

◦ Now let ϵ′ = ϵ − δ > 0.

◦ Verify that Iϵ′ (z) ⊆ Iϵ (x).
     ◦ Pick y ∈ Iϵ′ (z). We must show that |y − x| < ϵ.

     ◦ |y − x| ≤ |y − z| + |z − x|




                                                                5/16
                              Open Intervals



                z − ϵ′            z       z + ϵ′

                x−ϵ                   x                   x+ϵ


◦ Let δ = |z − x| > 0.

◦ Now let ϵ′ = ϵ − δ > 0.

◦ Verify that Iϵ′ (z) ⊆ Iϵ (x).
     ◦ Pick y ∈ Iϵ′ (z). We must show that |y − x| < ϵ.

     ◦ |y − x| ≤ |y − z| + |z − x| < ϵ′ + δ = ϵ.




                                                                5/16
                            Open Balls



Definition 1

 The open ball with center x ∈ Rn and radius ϵ > 0 is the set Bϵ (x)
 in Rn defined by
               Bϵ (x) ≡ {y ∈ Rn : ∥y − x∥ < ϵ} .




                                   ϵ
                                   x

It is possible to “capture” z inside an open ball fully contained in Bϵ (x).

 Exercise 1

   Show that Bϵ′ (z) is entirely contained in Bϵ (x).



                                                                               7/16
 Open and Closed Sets




                       8/16
                           Open Sets in Rn


Definition 2

 The set U ⊆ Rn is open if for each x ∈ U there exists an ϵ > 0 such
 that the open ball Bϵ (x) is entirely contained in U .




                                                                       9/16
                           Open Sets in Rn


Example 1

  (a) The interval (a, b) ⊆ R is open.

 (b) The half line (a, +∞) is also open.

  (c) The open ball Bϵ (x) ⊆ Rn is an open set.

 (d) The square (0, 1) × (0, 1) is an open subset of R2 .




                                                            10/16
                           Open Sets in Rn


Example 1

  (a) The interval (a, b) ⊆ R is open.

 (b) The half line (a, +∞) is also open.

  (c) The open ball Bϵ (x) ⊆ Rn is an open set.

 (d) The square (0, 1) × (0, 1) is an open subset of R2 .

  (e) What about [a, b], [a, b) and (−∞, b]?

  (f) What about the union or intersection of open sets?




                                                            10/16
                        Closed Sets in Rn

Definition 3

 A set V ⊆ Rn is closed if its complement V c = {x ∈ Rn : x ∈
                                                            / V } is
 open.




                                                                       11/16
                           Closed Sets in Rn

Definition 3

 A set V ⊆ Rn is closed if its complement V c = {x ∈ Rn : x ∈
                                                            / V } is
 open.



Example 2

  (a) The interval [a, b] ⊆ R is closed subset of R.

  (b) The half line (−∞, b] is also closed.

  (c) [a, b) is neither open nor closed.

  (d) The closed ball B ϵ (x) ⊆ Rn is a closed set.




                                                                       11/16
                       Open and Closed Sets



Proposition 1

  (a) Any arbitrary union of open sets is open.

  (b) Any finite intersection of open sets is open.

  (c) Any arbitrary intersection of closed sets is closed.

  (d) Any finite union of closed sets is closed.




                                                             12/16
                         Open and Closed Sets



 Proposition 1

    (a) Any arbitrary union of open sets is open.

    (b) Any finite intersection of open sets is open.

    (c) Any arbitrary intersection of closed sets is closed.

    (d) Any finite union of closed sets is closed.



Note that (c) and (d) follow from (a) and (b) using De Morgan’s Laws.

We’ll prove Proposition 1 after a few examples.

                        Proof of Proposition 1



Let A1 , A2 , A3 , . . . be an infinite collection of open sets.
                                                           S
(a) Let I ⊆ N be an index set. We show that A = i∈I Ai is open.

                        Proof of Proposition 1



Let A1 , A2 , A3 , . . . be an infinite collection of open sets.
                                                           S
(a) Let I ⊆ N be an index set. We show that A = i∈I Ai is open.
  ◦ Let x ∈ A be arbitrarily chosen.

  ◦ Then x ∈ Ai for some i ∈ I.




                                                                   14/16
                         Proof of Proposition 1



Let A1 , A2 , A3 , . . . be an infinite collection of open sets.
                                                           S
(a) Let I ⊆ N be an index set. We show that A = i∈I Ai is open.
  ◦ Let x ∈ A be arbitrarily chosen.

  ◦ Then x ∈ Ai for some i ∈ I.

  ◦ There exists an open ball Bϵ (x) contained in Ai .

  ◦ Thus, Bϵ (x) is contained in A.




                                                                   14/16

                            Proof of Proposition 1


Let A1 , A2 , A3 , . . . be an infinite collection of open sets.

(b) Let J ⊆ N be a finite index set. We show that A′ =
                                                                   T
                                                                       j∈J Aj is open.
                ′
  ◦ Let x ∈ A .

  ◦ Then x ∈ Aj , for all j ∈ J.

  ◦ There is an open ball Bϵj (x) contained in Aj , for all j ∈ J.

  ◦ Take ϵ = min{ϵj : j ∈ J} > 0.         (Here is where we need J to be finite.)

  ◦ The open ball Bϵ (x) is contained in Aj , for every j ∈ J.




                                                                                         15/16
                  Bounded and Compact Sets

Definition 4

 A set S ⊆ Rn is said to be bounded if there exists an open ball
 centered at 0 that contains S.

 A set C ⊆ Rn is said to be compact if it is closed and bounded.




                                                                   16/16
                    Bounded and Compact Sets

 Definition 4

  A set S ⊆ Rn is said to be bounded if there exists an open ball
  centered at 0 that contains S.

  A set C ⊆ Rn is said to be compact if it is closed and bounded.


Comments:
 ◦ Most textbooks on mathematical analysis define compact sets via open
   covers. In Euclidean spaces, this is equivalent to the definition given
   above.

 ◦ Compact sets are extremely important for optimization.




                                                                             16/16
